name: Quick Sticks Wall Ball
subtitle: Rapid-fire catch and release against a wall
description: Stand 3-5 yards from a sturdy wall. Catch and release the ball immediately without cradling. Focus on soft hands and a quick release.
coachingPoints: No cradling between catch and throw
Develop 'soft hands' — absorb the ball on the catch
Aim for a specific spot on the wall each rep
steps: Stand 3-5 yards from a brick or concrete wall
Throw the ball against the wall at chest height
Catch and immediately release without cradling
Complete 25-50 reps with dominant hand
Switch to non-dominant hand and repeat
commonMistakes: Cradling between catch and throw — defeats the purpose
Standing too far from the wall — reduces rep speed
Not switching hands

name: One-Handed Wall Ball
subtitle: Build wrist snap and forearm strength
description: Hold the stick with one hand at the balance point. Pass and catch against a wall using only wrist snap. Builds forearm strength and the fine motor control needed for quick releases.
coachingPoints: Use wrist snap only — no arm swing
Keep elbow relatively still
Builds the forearm strength critical for shooting power
steps: Stand 5 yards from the wall
Hold stick with one hand near the balance point (throat area)
Snap wrist to throw the ball against the wall
Catch with the same hand
Complete 25 reps per hand
commonMistakes: Using full arm motion instead of isolating wrist
Gripping too tightly — stay loose
Not completing reps on weak hand

name: Cross-Body (Canadian) Wall Ball
subtitle: Catch and throw on the opposite shoulder without switching hands
description: Throw and catch on the opposite side of your body without switching hands. For example, hold the stick right-handed but catch on your left shoulder. Simulates passing while protecting the stick through traffic.
coachingPoints: Keep the stick close to your body across the face
This mimics protecting the stick while passing in traffic
Focus on accuracy before speed
steps: Stand 5-7 yards from the wall
Hold stick right-handed but position it on your left shoulder
Throw and catch without switching hands
Complete 25 reps, then switch to left-handed on right shoulder
commonMistakes: Switching hands instead of keeping cross-body
Dropping the elbow — keep it up for a clean release

name: Lax Shuffle Wall Ball
subtitle: Pass and catch while shuffling laterally along the wall
description: Shuffle sideways along the wall while passing and catching. Simulates passing while moving through traffic and develops coordination between footwork and stick skills.
coachingPoints: Keep feet moving — never stop shuffling
Stay in an athletic stance, knees bent
Maintain accuracy while moving
steps: Stand 5 yards from the wall
Begin shuffling laterally (right)
Throw and catch while continuing to shuffle
After 10 reps, reverse direction (shuffle left)
Switch hands and repeat
commonMistakes: Stopping feet to throw — must stay in motion
Crossing feet during shuffle

name: Egg Toss
subtitle: Partner passing with increasing distance
description: In pairs, players start close and pass back and forth. Every successful catch allows them to take one giant step backward. The last pair to drop their 'egg' wins.
coachNotes: Great warm-up drill. Gets players laughing and competing early in practice.
coachingPoints: Step toward your target with your opposite foot
Follow through with stick pointed at your partner
Absorb the catch — don't stab at the ball
steps: Pair up players facing each other, 5 yards apart
Pass back and forth
On each successful catch, both take one step back
If the ball is dropped, that pair is eliminated
Last pair standing wins
commonMistakes: Throwing too hard at close distance
Not stepping with opposite foot

name: Star Drill
subtitle: Five-line passing drill with movement
description: Five lines form a star shape. Players pass to the line two spots away and then follow their pass to the end of that line. Requires high-level focus and communication.
coachNotes: Excellent team passing drill. Forces communication — players must call for the ball.
coachingPoints: Call for the ball before it arrives
Pass to the line two spots away (skip one line)
Follow your pass and join the back of that line
Keep your head on a swivel to avoid collisions
steps: Set up 5 cones in a star pattern, 15-20 yards apart
Place players in lines behind each cone
First player passes to the line two spots to their right
After passing, sprint to the end of the line they passed to
Receiving player catches, then passes to the line two spots away
Continue the pattern
commonMistakes: Passing to the wrong line (adjacent instead of two away)
Not following the pass
Not communicating — leads to collisions

name: 3-Man Weave
subtitle: Classic full-field passing and conditioning drill
description: Three players run down the field, passing the ball and weaving behind the player they just passed to. Excellent for conditioning, transition passing, and communication.
coachNotes: Run this drill at the start of practice as a warm-up with conditioning built in.
coachingPoints: Lead your receiver with the pass
Sprint behind the person you pass to
Finish with a shot on goal at the end
Maintain spacing — don't bunch up
steps: Three players line up across the field at the end line
Middle player starts with the ball
Pass to one side, then weave behind the receiver
Receiver passes to the third player, weaves behind them
Continue down the field
Finish with a shot on goal
commonMistakes: Not weaving behind the receiver — running straight
Bunching up — maintain 10-yard spacing
Slowing down after passing

name: Triangle Passing
subtitle: Three-player continuous passing in a triangle formation
description: Three players form a triangle about 10-15 yards apart. They pass in one direction (clockwise), then switch to the other (counter-clockwise) to work both hands.
coachingPoints: Catch with outside hand, pass with outside hand
Switch direction to work both hands equally
Step toward your target on every pass
steps: Set up 3 cones in a triangle, 10-15 yards apart
One player at each cone
Pass clockwise for 2 minutes
Switch to counter-clockwise for 2 minutes
Add a second ball for advanced groups
commonMistakes: Always using dominant hand — switch with direction change
Flat-footed catching — stay on balls of feet

name: Monkey in the Middle
subtitle: Keep-away passing game with a defender
description: A classic keep-away game in a 10x10 yard box. Two offensive players try to pass around one defender. If the defender touches the ball, they switch roles.
coachNotes: Great for youth — competitive and fun while teaching passing under pressure.
coachingPoints: Passers: use fakes before throwing
Defender: read the passer's eyes and stick position
Move your feet — don't stand flat-footed
steps: Set up a 10x10 yard box with cones
Two offensive players on opposite sides, one defender in the middle
Offensive players pass back and forth
If the defender touches or intercepts the ball, they switch with the passer
Rotate every 2 minutes regardless
commonMistakes: Passers standing still — move to create angles
Defender lunging instead of shuffling

name: Back-Up Contest
subtitle: Partner passing distance challenge
description: Partners start close together. After every successful catch, both players take one step back. If the ball is dropped, the pair is out or must start over.
coachingPoints: Technique matters more at longer distances
Step and follow through to generate distance
Absorb the catch with give in the arms
steps: Pair up facing each other, 3 yards apart
Pass back and forth
On each successful catch, both take one giant step back
Drop = elimination or restart
Last pair standing wins
commonMistakes: Muscling the throw at long distance — use proper technique
Stiff hands on the catch

name: Around the World (Spot Shooting)
subtitle: Shoot from 5 spots around the arc, targeting corners
description: Place 5 balls at 5 distinct spots around the 10-15 yard arc (wing, top left, top center, top right, opposite wing). Shoot all 5 balls sequentially, aiming for the exact same corner of the goal.
coachNotes: Start without a goalie to build confidence, then add one.
coachingPoints: Pick a specific spot — a link in the net mesh, not just 'the corner'
Ensure feet and shoulders are squared to the target before releasing
Repeat the circuit for each corner of the goal
steps: Place 5 balls at 5 spots around the arc: wing, top-left, top-center, top-right, opposite wing
Start at first spot, shoot aiming for top-right corner
Move to next spot and repeat
After completing all 5 spots, repeat for each corner (top-left, bottom-right, bottom-left)
commonMistakes: Aiming vaguely at 'the corner' instead of a precise spot
Rushing the shot — set your feet first
Not following through

name: One-Handed Form Shooting
subtitle: Isolate wrist snap for accuracy
description: Stand approximately 5 yards from the goal with no goalie. Hold the stick with only your top hand at the balance point. Snap your wrist to shoot the ball into the corners.
coachNotes: This is a technique drill, not a power drill. Focus on precision.
coachingPoints: Isolate wrist snap and elbow placement
Keep shooting elbow high and away from your body
Maintain a vertical overhand release
steps: Stand 5 yards from the goal (no goalie)
Hold stick with only your top (dominant) hand at the balance point
Snap wrist to shoot into corners
Focus on top-right, top-left, bottom-right, bottom-left in sequence
Switch to non-dominant hand
commonMistakes: Using arm instead of wrist — isolate the snap
Dropping the elbow
Shooting with too much power — this is about accuracy

name: Shooting on the Run (Alley Drill)
subtitle: Full-speed dodge and shoot down the alley
description: Start at a cone at the top of the box. Dodge 'down the alley' (the side of the field) at full speed and shoot while moving. Aim for the far pipe.
coachNotes: Emphasize that power comes from core rotation, not arm strength.
coachingPoints: Get hands back early and away from body as you run
Generate power through core torque, not just arms
Rotate hips toward the goal just before release
Aim for the far pipe — shoot across the goalie's body
steps: Place a cone at the top of the box
Line up behind the cone with a ball
Sprint down the alley at full speed
Shoot while moving — do not stop your feet
Aim for the far pipe
commonMistakes: Slowing down or stopping to shoot
Dropping hands before the shot
Not rotating hips — arm-only shot lacks power

name: Hitch and Go
subtitle: Pump fake into a sprint and shot
description: Receive a pass, perform a hard pump fake (hitch), then sprint toward the middle of the field for a shot. Teaches deception and quick release.
coachingPoints: Sell the fake — the hitch must be dramatic enough to move a defender
Quick release after the hitch — unload immediately
Simulate beating a sliding defender
steps: Line of players at the wing or top, with a feeder
Receive a pass from the feeder
Perform a hard pump fake (full shooting motion without releasing)
Sprint toward the middle of the field
Shoot on the run
commonMistakes: Weak fake — defender doesn't bite
Too much time between fake and shot
Running laterally instead of attacking the cage

name: Over-Goal Shooting Drill
subtitle: Force overhand technique by shooting over an obstacle goal
description: Place one goal about 20 yards in front of the target goal. Stand behind the obstacle goal and shoot over the top of it into the target net. Forces proper overhand mechanics.
coachNotes: Great drill for players who tend to shoot sidearm.
coachingPoints: Forces high release point — sidearm shots hit the obstacle
Focus on extended arm position to clear the obstacle
Maintain power despite the high release
steps: Place an obstacle goal 20 yards in front of the target goal
Stand behind the obstacle goal
Shoot over the top of it into the target net
If ball hits the obstacle, your release is too low
Aim for corners of the target goal
commonMistakes: Dropping to sidearm — the obstacle catches it
Not extending arms fully
Losing accuracy while trying to clear the obstacle

name: Pipe City
subtitle: Aim for the pipe to train precision shooting
description: Take shots from 10-12 yards out, aiming specifically to hit the metal pipe of the goal or a bucket/cone placed right next to it. The 'ping' confirms elite accuracy.
coachNotes: Make it competitive — who can hit the pipe the most in 10 shots?
coachingPoints: Hearing the 'ping' confirms the shot is in the hardest spot for a goalie
Change planes — if you catch high, shoot low; catch low, shoot high
This is the smallest target you can aim for
steps: Stand 10-12 yards from the goal
Aim to hit the pipe (top bar, left pipe, or right pipe)
Take 10 shots per round
Track hits — make it a competition
Vary your shooting position (standing, on the run, off a dodge)
commonMistakes: Getting frustrated — this is intentionally difficult
Sacrificing form for power

name: Ground Ball Rodeo
subtitle: Chaotic scramble for ground balls under pressure
description: Multiple players fight for one ball in a tight space. The winner must scoop and clear the ball to a coach. Simulates game-speed ground ball battles.
coachNotes: High energy drill — great for building toughness. Keep reps short.
coachingPoints: Two butts low — stick butt and player butt
Box out before scooping
Run through the ball and accelerate away from the scrum
steps: Create a 10x10 yard box with cones
3-4 players inside the box
Coach rolls or throws a ball into the box
Players fight for the ground ball
Winner must scoop and pass to the coach outside the box
Reset and repeat
commonMistakes: Standing upright — must stay low
Scooping and standing still — must accelerate away
Reaching instead of getting body over the ball

name: Man-Ball Drill
subtitle: Partner ground ball with designated roles
description: Two players approach a ground ball. One 'takes the man' (body check/box out) while the other 'takes the ball.' Teaches teamwork on 50/50 ground balls.
coachNotes: Critical fundamental for winning the ground ball battle. The 'man' player must communicate.
coachingPoints: Communication: yell 'Man!' or 'Ball!' before arriving
'Man' player uses hips and shoulders to shield opponent
'Ball' player scoops in stride and accelerates away
Top hand high on the scoop for maximum control
steps: Two pairs of players line up 10 yards from a ground ball
Coach blows whistle
One player from each pair calls 'Man!' (blocks) and the other calls 'Ball!' (scoops)
Execute the roles
Ball player must complete a pass to a coach/teammate after scooping
commonMistakes: Both players going for the ball — someone must take the man
Not communicating before arriving
Man player going too high — stay low and box out

name: Plus 1 Drill
subtitle: 2v2 ground ball scramble with a late-entering 5th player
description: A 2v2 scramble for a ground ball. A 5th player (the '+1') enters to help the team that wins the scoop, creating a numbers advantage situation.
coachNotes: Adds transition element to ground ball work. The +1 simulates a late-breaking midfielder.
coachingPoints: Win the ground ball first — everything starts there
+1 player must read who wins and sprint to support
Losing team must immediately transition to defense
steps: Two pairs of players line up on opposite sides
Coach rolls a ground ball between them
2v2 scramble for the ball
5th player (+1) enters from the sideline to join the team that wins possession
Play 3v2 to goal
Reset and rotate
commonMistakes: +1 entering too early before possession is decided
Losing team not transitioning to defense quickly

name: Hungry Hungry Hippos
subtitle: Fun youth ground ball collection race
description: Scatter all team balls in the center of a 20x20 yard grid. On the whistle, players race to scoop one ball at a time and return it to their 'nest' (a bucket or designated cone). The player or team with the most balls wins.
coachNotes: Kids love this drill. Disguises high-rep ground ball work as a game.
coachingPoints: Scoop through the ball — don't stab at it
Butt to the grass on every scoop
One ball at a time — no carrying multiples
steps: Set up a 20x20 yard grid with cones
Scatter 20-30 balls in the center
Players start at edges or assigned 'nests' (cones/buckets)
On whistle, race to scoop one ball and bring it back to nest
Return for another ball immediately
Player/team with the most balls when the center is empty wins
commonMistakes: Trying to carry multiple balls
Not using proper scoop technique in the rush
Standing upright — stay low

name: Messy Backyard
subtitle: Team ground ball race — clean your side
description: Divide players into two teams with a 'backyard' grid in between. Each team must scoop balls from their side and deposit them into the opponent's side. After 60 seconds, the team with the cleanest backyard wins.
coachNotes: Another great disguised ground ball drill for youth. High energy, lots of reps.
coachingPoints: Proper scoop form even in the chaos
Scoop and place — don't just throw the ball
Keep your head up to avoid collisions
steps: Divide a 20x20 yard grid in half
Scatter equal numbers of balls on each side
Two teams, one on each side
On whistle, scoop balls from your side and deposit on opponent's side
60-second timer
Team with fewer balls on their side wins
commonMistakes: Throwing balls instead of placing them on the other side
Poor scoop technique in the rush

name: Diamond Drill (1v1 Defense)
subtitle: Defender approaches ball carrier at speed
description: Four cones in a 10-yard diamond. Attacker and defender sprint to side cones, then meet at top. Coach feeds the ball and they play live 1v1. Teaches defensive approach and breakdown.
coachingPoints: Break down (short choppy steps) before contact
Stay balanced — don't lunge
Use stick to disrupt the dodger's vision
Drive the attacker toward the sideline
steps: Set up 4 cones in a 10-yard diamond shape
Attacker starts at one side cone, defender at the other
Both sprint to the top cone
Coach feeds the ball to the attacker as they arrive
Play live 1v1 to the goal
Rotate roles
commonMistakes: Defender arriving flat-footed instead of broken down
Overcommitting to the stick check
Not staying between attacker and the goal

name: Zig-Zag Defensive Footwork
subtitle: Lateral shuffle drill for body positioning
description: Set 7-8 cones in a zig-zag pattern. The defender moves backward using lateral shuffles, keeping chest square and stick up. Trains influence and sideline positioning.
coachNotes: Fundamental footwork drill. Can be done individually or in groups.
coachingPoints: Never cross your feet — always shuffle
Keep chest square to the imaginary attacker
Stick up and active — don't let it drag
Stay in an athletic stance, hips low
steps: Set 7-8 cones in a zig-zag pattern, 5 yards apart
Start at the first cone in defensive stance
Shuffle laterally to the next cone
Change direction at each cone
Keep stick up and active throughout
Complete 3-5 reps
commonMistakes: Crossing feet — always shuffle
Standing too upright
Dropping the stick between cones

name: NASCAR Drill
subtitle: Sprint to close out on a moving dodger
description: Defender starts near the goal and sprints out to a dodger who is already moving. The goal is to 'break down' (short, choppy steps) just before contact to avoid being blown by.
coachNotes: Critical for teaching the approach. Most goals are scored when the defender arrives too fast and gets dodged.
coachingPoints: Sprint to close the gap, then break down 3-4 yards out
Short choppy steps on the approach — don't lunge
Use stick to disrupt vision, not wild checks
Force the attacker to their weak hand
steps: Defender starts at the crease area
Attacker starts at midfield, already moving toward the goal
On whistle, defender sprints to close out
Defender must break down before reaching the attacker
Play live 1v1
Rotate
commonMistakes: Not breaking down — arriving at full speed and getting dodged
Lunging with the stick
Not getting hip-to-hip with the attacker

name: Salisbury Call Drill (Slide and Recover)
subtitle: Team defensive slide rotation on command
description: Fast-paced drill where a coach calls a trigger. One defender slides to the ball, remaining defenders communicate 'Two' (second slide) and 'Bump' roles. Practices the recovery phase.
coachNotes: This is the backbone of team defense. Run it every practice.
coachingPoints: Communication is everything — call 'Ball!', 'Hot!', 'Two!', 'Fire!'
Slide to where the dodger is going, not where they are
The beaten defender must recover to the 'hole' to find a new man
Maintain slide tunnels — clear paths to the ball
steps: Set up 5-6 offensive players around the perimeter (stationary or passing)
4-5 defenders in a man-down or even-strength configuration
Coach calls a 'trigger' — defender on ball yells 'Ball!'
Adjacent defender slides ('Hot!') and calls 'Fire!'
Next defender rotates to cover ('Two!')
Original beaten defender sprints to the hole and finds the open man
Reset and repeat from different positions
commonMistakes: Silent defense — must communicate constantly
Sliding too late or too early
Not recovering after sliding — leaves a man open

name: 5-Cone Slide and Recover
subtitle: Continuous defensive rotation in a circle
description: Arrange 5 cones in a circle with 4 offensive players and 3 defenders. As offense passes the ball, 3 defenders must constantly rotate: one on ball, one 'Hot', one 'Two'.
coachNotes: Emphasizes that defense is a moving unit. When one slides, everyone rotates.
coachingPoints: Defense is a unit — when one moves, everyone adjusts
Always know who is 'Hot' and who is 'Two'
Keep sticks up in passing lanes
Rotate together like a string
steps: Place 5 cones in a circle, 10 yards apart
4 offensive players stand at 4 of the cones
3 defenders inside the circle
Offense passes the ball around the circle
Defenders rotate: one pressures ball, one is Hot, one is Two
Every pass triggers a rotation
Run for 2-3 minutes, then swap groups
commonMistakes: Ball-watching instead of covering your rotation
Not talking — silent defense gets scored on
Over-committing to the ball — losing your secondary assignment

name: Chop Drill (Face-Off)
subtitle: Isolate the first punch motion for hand speed
description: Perform just the first 'punch' motion of a clamp repeatedly on a whistle to isolate reaction speed. Pure hand speed training for face-off specialists.
coachNotes: High-rep drill. Focus on clean technique, not just speed.
coachingPoints: Isolate just the first punch — don't complete the full clamp
Drive top hand down and across
Keep weight balanced — don't lean forward onto hands
steps: Set up in face-off stance (Standing Neutral Grip)
On whistle, perform just the initial punch/clamp motion
Reset to starting position
Repeat for 10-20 reps per set
Rest 30 seconds between sets
commonMistakes: Anticipating the whistle — must react
Leaning weight onto hands before the whistle
Completing the full exit instead of isolating the punch

name: Resistance Band Clamps
subtitle: Build explosive clamp power with resistance
description: Use a resistance band anchored to a fence or held by a partner to build the 'snap' muscles in your forearms. Perform clamp motions against resistance.
coachNotes: Strength-building drill. Don't sacrifice form for reps.
coachingPoints: Maintain proper SNG stance throughout
Focus on explosive snap against the resistance
Control the return — don't let the band snap your hands back
steps: Anchor a resistance band to a fence or have a partner hold it
Attach band to your stick or hands
Set up in face-off stance
Perform clamp motion against the resistance
10-15 reps per set, 3-4 sets
commonMistakes: Using too much resistance — start light
Losing proper stance under load
Jerky motions instead of smooth, explosive snaps

name: Whistle Cadence Drill
subtitle: Unpredictable timing for face-off reaction training
description: Use a whistle app or partner with unpredictable timing (holding 'Set' for 1-5 seconds) to prevent anticipating. Trains pure reaction to the whistle.
coachNotes: The randomized timing is critical — muscle memory must be triggered by sound, not anticipation.
coachingPoints: Stay relaxed before the whistle — tension slows reaction
React to the sound, not to anticipation
Vary the hold time from 1-5 seconds
steps: Set up in face-off stance
Partner or coach calls 'Down... Set...' with random timing
On whistle, execute your primary move (clamp, rake, etc.)
Reset and repeat
15-20 reps per session
commonMistakes: Anticipating the whistle and false-starting
Not varying the hold time enough
Tensing up during the hold — stay loose

name: 50/50 Tie-Up Drill
subtitle: Battle for the ball from a contested clamp position
description: Start with both sticks already clamped on the ball. On the whistle, fight to win the 'exit' using hip rotation and leverage. Practices winning the battle after the initial clamp.
coachNotes: Many face-offs come down to the tie-up. This drill isolates that critical moment.
coachingPoints: Use hip rotation and leverage, not just arm strength
Stay low — center of gravity wins
Practice multiple exit moves (pop out, push through)
steps: Two face-off players set up with sticks clamped on the ball
On whistle, both fight to win possession
Winner must cleanly exit with the ball
Best of 5, then rotate partners
commonMistakes: Standing too upright — stay low
Using only arms — hips and core generate the power
Not practicing counter moves when your primary fails

name: Banana Curl Breakout
subtitle: Clearing drill with curved sprint path
description: Upon a goalie save or 'Clear' call, defenders sprint toward the sideline in a curved 'banana' path. This opens their hips to the goalie, providing a chest-pass target.
coachNotes: Fundamental clear pattern. The curve is what makes the pass easy — running straight away forces an over-the-shoulder catch.
coachingPoints: Run a curved path — not straight — to open hips to the goalie
Present a chest-pass target, not an over-the-shoulder target
Look back to the goalie as you curl
Sprint after catching — don't jog
steps: Goalie starts with the ball in the crease
Defenders start in the box area
On 'Clear!' call, defenders sprint toward sidelines in curved banana paths
Goalie reads the field and hits the most open defender
Defender catches and transitions up field
Add riding pressure for advanced reps
commonMistakes: Running straight away from the goalie — must curve
Not looking back for the pass
Jogging instead of sprinting after the catch

name: Goalie Z-Pattern Scan
subtitle: Goalie reads clearing options in priority order
description: The goalie scans the field in a 'Z' shape after a save: first across midline for a fast break, then across the top of the box for a delayed break, then to the wings at GLE.
coachNotes: The goalie is the quarterback of the clear. This drill teaches the read progression.
coachingPoints: Z-pattern: midline first (fast break), then box top (delayed), then wings (GLE)
Read the whole field before committing to a pass
Hit the first open man — don't hold the ball
Strong outlet pass — goalies must throw accurately
steps: Goalie makes a save or starts with the ball
Scan the field in a Z-pattern: far midfield → near midfield → far wing → near wing
Clearing players position themselves at these spots
Coach designates which player is 'open' each rep
Goalie must find and hit the open man
Vary which player is open each time
commonMistakes: Tunnel vision — only looking at one option
Holding the ball too long — creates riding pressure
Weak passes that get picked off

name: 1v1 Revenge Ride
subtitle: Immediate transition from offense to riding
description: Following a shot or turnover, the original offensive player immediately becomes the rider. They must ride the clearing player toward the sideline, using the boundary as an extra defender.
coachNotes: Teaches the mentality of immediate transition. Attack players must ride hard.
coachingPoints: Transition mentally from offense to defense instantly
Use the sideline as an extra defender — push the clearer there
Sprint to get in front of the clearing player
Stick in passing lanes to disrupt the clear
steps: Attacker takes a shot on goal
Goalie makes the save and begins clearing to a defender
The attacker immediately becomes the rider
Ride the clearing defender 1v1
Force toward the sideline or cause a turnover
Play resets after a successful clear or turnover
commonMistakes: Standing and watching after the shot — must transition immediately
Chasing instead of angling off the clearing player
Not using the sideline as leverage

name: West Genny (3v2 Fast Break)
subtitle: Continuous full-field transition drill
description: A continuous full-field drill where a 3v2 starts at one end. After the shot, the shooter and two defenders exit while remaining players become defenders as 3 new offensive players enter from the sideline.
coachNotes: One of the best all-around drills in lacrosse. Combines conditioning, transition, fast breaks, and decision-making.
coachingPoints: Move the ball quickly — don't overdodge
Find the open man in the fast break
Transition immediately from offense to defense
Communicate: who's hot, who's covering who
steps: Split team into groups of 3 on each sideline
Start with a 3v2 at one end
After the play ends (goal, save, or turnover), the 3 offensive players sprint off
2 defenders plus 1 new player become the next offensive group of 3
3 new defenders enter from the other sideline
Continuous flow — keep the drill moving
commonMistakes: Slow transitions — must sprint on and off
Overdodging instead of passing in the fast break
Defenders not communicating matchups

name: 4v3 Scramble
subtitle: Ground ball to numbers-up transition
description: Coach rolls a ball into a group of 3 middies. Winner enters the offensive zone to create a 4v3 (with 3 attack and 3 poles already in place). Forces quick decision-making with numbers advantage.
coachNotes: Combines ground balls with unsettled transition. Game-like reps.
coachingPoints: Win the ground ball first — everything starts there
Sprint to create the 4v3 advantage quickly
Move the ball — the advantage disappears as defense recovers
Identify where the slide is coming from
steps: 3 attack players and 3 defensemen set up in the offensive zone
3 midfielders line up at midfield
Coach rolls a ground ball between the middies
Winner sprints to offensive zone creating 4v3
Losers sprint back to play defense (or sit out)
Play until a goal, save, or turnover
commonMistakes: Holding the ball too long — defense is recovering
Not recognizing where the open man is
Midfielders not sprinting after the ground ball result

name: Walk the Arc
subtitle: Goalie positioning and movement drill
description: Set up 5 cones on the arc (left pipe, left 45°, center, right 45°, right pipe). Practice moving between them using drop steps and jump stops to land balanced at each position.
coachNotes: Fundamental goalie drill. Must be perfect before adding shots.
coachingPoints: Use a drop step (open hip) to move, followed by a jump stop to land balanced
Stay square to the imaginary ball at each cone
Keep hands up in ready position at all times
Move along the arc, not straight across the crease
steps: Set up 5 cones on the goalie arc: left pipe, left 45°, center, right 45°, right pipe
Start at center cone in ready stance
Drop step to left 45° cone, jump stop, set
Drop step to left pipe, jump stop, set
Return to center, then repeat to the right side
Increase speed as form improves
commonMistakes: Crossing feet — always use drop step
Moving in a straight line instead of following the arc
Dropping hands during movement
Not being set (balanced) when arriving at each position

name: Numbered Arc Drill
subtitle: Goalie reacts to called positions on the arc
description: Coach calls out a number (1-5) representing a spot on the arc. Goalie must sprint or shuffle to that spot and land perfectly set. Builds reaction and positioning under pressure.
coachNotes: Add shots after arrival to combine positioning with saves.
coachingPoints: Be set before the shot comes — positioning first
Sprint to the spot, then jump stop to balance
Keep hands up and ready even while moving
steps: Number the 5 arc positions 1 through 5
Goalie starts at center (position 3)
Coach calls a random number
Goalie sprints/shuffles to that position and sets
Coach can add a shot immediately after arrival
Increase speed of calls as goalie improves
commonMistakes: Not being set before the shot arrives
Moving too slowly between positions
Cheating by anticipating the next call

name: Colored Ball Reaction Drill
subtitle: Goalie calls color while making the save
description: Coach tosses tennis balls of different colors. Goalie must shout the color while driving their top hand and lead foot to make the save. Combines visual processing with save mechanics.
coachNotes: Forces the goalie to track the ball all the way in. The color call proves they're watching.
coachingPoints: Call the color BEFORE making the save — proves you're tracking
Use proper lead hand, lead step mechanics
Keep eyes on the ball through the entire flight
steps: Coach stands 10-15 yards out with a bucket of different colored tennis balls
Toss a ball at the goalie at moderate speed
Goalie must call the color AND make the save
Increase speed and vary locations
20-30 reps per session
commonMistakes: Making the save without calling the color — not tracking properly
Guessing the color instead of actually seeing it
Reverting to bad mechanics under cognitive load

name: Reaction Ball Drill
subtitle: Unpredictable bounces for goalie reaction training
description: Use a six-sided reaction ball against a wall. Its unpredictable bounce forces the goalie to react to the ball's actual movement rather than a pre-determined path.
coachNotes: Can be done solo. Great for pre-practice warm-up.
coachingPoints: Stay in athletic stance — ready to move any direction
React to what you see, not what you expect
Use proper save mechanics even on wild bounces
steps: Stand 5-8 feet from a wall
Throw the reaction ball at the wall
React to the unpredictable bounce and catch/save it
Vary throw speed and angle
3-5 minute sessions
commonMistakes: Standing flat-footed — stay on balls of feet
Only using hands — move your whole body
Throwing too hard at first — build up speed

name: Shell Drill (Man-Up/Man-Down)
subtitle: Slow-motion defensive rotation for EMO defense
description: A stationary or slow-moving 5v5 or 6v5 drill focusing solely on defensive rotations as the ball is passed around the perimeter. Teaches zone defense principles.
coachNotes: Teach at walk-through speed first. Add tempo once rotations are clean.
coachingPoints: Keep sticks up in passing lanes at all times
Rotate as a string — when one moves, all adjust
Identify the cutter and communicate
Force shots from outside and low-angle positions
steps: 6 offensive players set up in man-up formation (e.g., 3-3)
5 defenders in man-down zone
Offense passes the ball around the perimeter (no dodging initially)
Defense rotates with each pass
Coach pauses to correct positioning
Gradually add dodging and live play
commonMistakes: Ball-watching — must track both ball and assigned zone
Not keeping sticks up in passing lanes
Over-rotating and leaving backside open

name: Knock-Down Drill
subtitle: Defenders intercept cross-crease passes
description: Coach fires passes across the crease area. Defenders must sprint to tip or intercept the passes. Builds the instinct to get sticks in passing lanes during man-down defense.
coachNotes: Every tipped or intercepted pass in man-down is a huge play. This drill builds that reflex.
coachingPoints: Keep stick up and active — anticipate the passing lane
Sprint to the lane, don't reach
Even a tip that prevents a catch is a win
steps: 2-3 defenders set up in the crease area
Coach stands at wing or top with balls
Coach fires passes across the crease (varying height and speed)
Defenders sprint to tip or intercept
Track successful knock-downs per round
20-30 pass attempts per session
commonMistakes: Reaching with one hand instead of moving body to the lane
Dropping stick between passes
Not resetting position after each attempt

name: Numbers Game (Man-Up/Man-Down)
subtitle: Variable numbers advantage game drill
description: Coach calls numbers to create variable advantages (2v1, 3v2, 4v3, 5v4, 6v5). Players sprint in from the sideline when their number is called. Teaches both man-up offense and man-down defense.
coachNotes: All-time favorite drill for youth through college. Keeps everyone engaged.
coachingPoints: Offense: move the ball quickly to find the open man
Defense: communicate and rotate as a unit
Be ready — your number could be called at any time
Sprint onto the field — simulates game urgency
steps: Assign numbers to players (1 through 6)
Offense on one sideline, defense on the other
Coach calls 'Offense 3, Defense 2' (creating a 3v2)
Those players sprint onto the field
Coach puts ball in play
Play until goal, save, or turnover
Coach calls new numbers for next rep — vary the advantage
commonMistakes: Not sprinting when number is called — jog = bench
Offense holding ball instead of moving it in advantage
Defense panicking instead of communicating

name: 5-10-5 Pro Agility Shuttle
subtitle: Classic agility test for change of direction
description: Three cones 5 yards apart in a line. Start at center, sprint 5 yards right (touch), sprint 10 yards left (touch), sprint 5 yards back to center.
coachNotes: Standard combine test. Time players and track improvement over the season.
coachingPoints: Stay low on the turns — don't stand up
Plant the outside foot hard to change direction
Explode out of each turn
Pump arms for acceleration
steps: Place 3 cones in a line, 5 yards apart
Start at the middle cone in athletic stance
On whistle, sprint 5 yards to the right cone (touch the line)
Sprint 10 yards to the left cone (touch the line)
Sprint 5 yards back to the center cone
Time each rep
commonMistakes: Standing too tall on direction changes
Not touching the line on each turn
Decelerating before the finish

name: T-Drill
subtitle: Multi-directional agility drill
description: Cones in a T shape (5 yards apart). Sprint forward to center, shuffle left, shuffle across to far right, shuffle back to center, backpedal to start.
coachNotes: Covers all four movement directions. Great for defenders especially.
coachingPoints: Sprint forward, shuffle laterally, backpedal — don't turn and run
Stay in athletic stance throughout
Touch each cone with your hand
Do with stick in hand for sport-specificity
steps: Set up 4 cones in a T shape, each 5 yards apart
Start at the bottom of the T
Sprint forward to the center cone (touch)
Shuffle left to the end cone (touch)
Shuffle right across to the far end cone (touch)
Shuffle back to center (touch)
Backpedal to start
commonMistakes: Turning hips to run instead of shuffling laterally
Not touching cones
Standing upright during shuffles

name: 300-Yard Shuttle
subtitle: Anaerobic endurance test
description: Sprint 25 yards and back 6 times (300 yards total). Aim to finish under 60 seconds with 2-3 minutes rest between sets. Simulates the anaerobic demands of lacrosse.
coachNotes: Standard lacrosse conditioning test. Target times: <54 seconds for varsity, <60 for JV.
coachingPoints: Pace yourself — don't go 100% on the first two sprints
Stay low on the turns
Push through the last 2 reps when legs are heavy
Full rest between sets — this is not a jog recovery
steps: Set up 2 cones 25 yards apart
Sprint from cone A to cone B and back (50 yards)
Repeat 6 times without stopping (300 yards total)
Record time
Rest 2-3 minutes
Repeat for 2-4 sets total
commonMistakes: Going too hard on the first rep and dying on the last
Not touching the line on turns
Not resting enough between sets

name: Full-Field Middie Intervals
subtitle: 110-yard sprint intervals for midfielders
description: Sprint the full length of the field (110 yards), rest for 30-45 seconds, and repeat 6-10 times. Simulates the transition demands on midfielders.
coachNotes: Midfielders run the most in lacrosse. This drill builds the engine they need.
coachingPoints: Full speed — this simulates a full-field transition
Active rest — walk/jog during the 30-45 second rest
Maintain form even when fatigued
Carry a stick for sport-specific conditioning
steps: Line up on one end line
Sprint 110 yards to the opposite end line
Rest 30-45 seconds (walk back to midfield)
Sprint again
Repeat 6-10 times
Track times to monitor conditioning
commonMistakes: Jogging instead of sprinting
Too much rest between reps
Poor form when fatigued — leads to injury

name: Split Dodge Cone Drill
subtitle: Isolate the split dodge with cone defenders
description: Set up cones 5-10 yards apart. Sprint toward the first cone, plant outside foot hard, switch hands across your face, and explode toward the second cone.
coachNotes: The split dodge is the bread-and-butter move. Every player must master it.
coachingPoints: Plant the outside foot hard — this sells the fake
Switch hands quickly across the face
Explode out of the dodge — change of speed is critical
Protect the stick on the switch
steps: Set up 2 cones 5-10 yards apart
Sprint toward the first cone with ball in right hand
Plant right foot hard at the cone
Switch hands (right to left) across your face
Explode toward the second cone
Repeat going back, switching left to right
commonMistakes: Slowing down before the dodge — must maintain speed
Switching hands below the waist — keep it across the face
Not changing speed after the dodge

name: Roll Dodge Drill
subtitle: Practice the spin move with a defender or obstacle
description: Run toward a defender or heavy pole/obstacle. Plant lead foot between the defender's feet, roll your back across their chest, and keep stick tucked tight to ear to prevent trail checks.
coachNotes: Use a trash can or blocking pad as a 'defender' for solo reps.
coachingPoints: Plant lead foot between defender's feet — get tight
Roll your back across the defender — use your body as a shield
Keep stick tucked to your ear — prevents trail checks
Accelerate out of the roll — don't spin and stand
steps: Approach a stationary defender or obstacle at 3/4 speed
Plant lead foot between/beside the defender
Spin (roll) keeping back toward the defender
Tuck stick tight to ear throughout the rotation
Explode out of the roll toward the goal
Progress to live 1v1 once technique is clean
commonMistakes: Starting the roll too far from the defender
Extending stick away from body during the spin
Not accelerating out of the roll

name: 1v1 Island Drill
subtitle: Confined 1v1 dodging in a small box
description: Attacker and defender start in a 5x5 yard box. Attacker must use one of the 4 core dodges to get past. Teaches real-time reading of defensive positioning and stick pressure.
coachNotes: The small space forces quick decisions. Great for both attackers and defenders.
coachingPoints: Attacker: read the defender's stance and react
Defender: stay balanced, don't overcommit
If defender is square, split dodge
If defender is tight, roll dodge
If defender's stick is high, face dodge underneath
steps: Create a 5x5 yard box with cones
Attacker starts at one end with the ball
Defender starts in the middle
Attacker must dodge past the defender to reach the other side (or shoot)
Play is dead if ball goes out of the box
Rotate after 3-5 reps per attacker
commonMistakes: Attacker using the same dodge every time — must read and react
Defender lunging instead of maintaining position
Not finishing after the dodge — must attack the cage

name: Sharks and Minnows
subtitle: Youth cradling game with defenders
description: Minnows (players with balls) must cradle from one end line to the other. Sharks (defenders/coaches) try to poke the ball away or tag them. If a minnow loses their ball, they become a shark.
coachNotes: Youth favorite. Gets kids running, cradling, and dodging with max engagement.
coachingPoints: Protect the stick — keep it on your outside hip
Change speed and direction to avoid sharks
Keep your head up — see the open lanes
steps: All players start on one end line with a ball (minnows)
1-3 players start as sharks in the middle (no ball)
On whistle, minnows run to the other end line while cradling
Sharks try to knock the ball away or tag minnows
If you lose your ball, you become a shark
Last minnow standing wins
commonMistakes: Minnows not protecting their stick
Running in a straight line — dodge the sharks
Sharks chasing instead of positioning

name: Pinnie Tag
subtitle: Cradling while protecting a tail flag
description: Every player has a ball and a pinnie tucked into their waistband like a tail. While cradling, they must try to pull others' pinnies while protecting their own.
coachNotes: Multitasking drill disguised as a game. Builds spatial awareness while cradling.
coachingPoints: Must cradle the ball the entire time — can't hold it in the pocket
Use body positioning to shield your pinnie
Keep your head on a swivel — offense and defense simultaneously
steps: Define a playing area (20x20 yard box)
Each player tucks a pinnie into the back of their waistband
Each player carries a ball in their stick
On whistle, try to pull others' pinnies while keeping your own
If you lose your ball OR your pinnie, you're out
Last player standing wins
commonMistakes: Forgetting to cradle while focused on grabbing pinnies
Turning your back too much — leaves your pinnie exposed

name: Castle Defender
subtitle: Shoot to topple a cone pyramid in the goal
description: Stack five cones in a pyramid inside the goal. Players take turns shooting to topple the castle. Award points for hitting specific cones or corners.
coachNotes: Makes shooting practice fun for young players. They'll take 50 shots without realizing it.
coachingPoints: Aim for specific cones — precision over power
Use proper shooting form even in a game setting
Follow through on every shot
steps: Stack 5 cones in a pyramid inside the goal (3 on bottom, 2 on top)
Players line up 10-12 yards out
Take turns shooting at the cone pyramid
Points for each cone knocked down
Bonus points for specific targets (top cone = 3 points)
Reset pyramid after each round
commonMistakes: Blasting shots without aiming
Poor form — still enforce proper mechanics
Shooting from too close or too far

name: Draw Control Boxing Out
subtitle: Women's lacrosse draw circle positioning drill
description: Coaches stand on the draw line and throw ground balls or high balls to two opponents on the circle. Players must use their bodies to box out the opponent and gain possession.
coachNotes: Draw controls are possession changers. Winning the draw is like winning a face-off.
coachingPoints: Position body between opponent and the ball
Box out with hips and shoulders
Track the ball in the air — hand-eye coordination is key
Communicate with circle players
steps: Two players set up on opposite sides of the draw circle
Coach stands at the center line
Coach throws a ball high in the air or rolls it on the ground
Players compete to box out and gain possession
Winner must complete a pass to a teammate
Rotate partners every 3 reps
commonMistakes: Not boxing out — going straight for the ball
Losing the ball after the initial win due to poor cradling
Not communicating with circle teammates

name: 8-Meter Arc Clearing Drill
subtitle: Women's lacrosse free position shooting space practice
description: Practice resetting after a foul where players must clear the arc 'like spokes on a wheel' to ensure the shooter has a safe, unobstructed path for a free position shot.
coachNotes: Shooting space violations are common. This drill prevents them.
coachingPoints: Clear the arc like spokes on a wheel — spread out evenly
Give the shooter a clear, unobstructed path to the goal
Move quickly — the shooter wants to go before defense resets
steps: Simulate a foul inside the 8-meter arc
Shooter places ball on the 8-meter mark
All other players must clear the arc
Clear 'like spokes on a wheel' — even spacing
Shooter takes the free position shot
Repeat from different positions around the arc
commonMistakes: Players bunching up while clearing
Clearing too slowly — gives defense time to set up
Shooter not being ready when the arc is clear

name: Quadrant 4v4 (Creating Space)
subtitle: Women's lacrosse spacing drill in the CSA
description: Divide the critical scoring area into four quadrants. Players must stay within their assigned quadrant while trying to score, forcing proper spacing and passing lanes.
coachNotes: Solves the number one youth problem: everyone running to the ball. Forces spacing.
coachingPoints: Stay in your quadrant — trust your teammates
Create passing lanes within the spacing structure
Off-ball movement within your quadrant to get open
Defense must also stay disciplined in coverage
steps: Divide the CSA into 4 quadrants with cones
4v4, each player assigned a quadrant
Players must stay in their quadrant
Play to score — emphasize ball movement and spacing
Rotate quadrants every 3-4 minutes
commonMistakes: Leaving your quadrant to 'help' — trust the system
Not moving within the quadrant — can still cut and dodge
Defenders sagging to the ball and leaving their quadrant

name: Bucket Drill (Box Lacrosse)
subtitle: Box lacrosse shooting on the move
description: Place a bucket or pylon 5-6 meters in front of the goal. Two lines at GLE. Players scoop a ball, run around the bucket to center themselves, and shoot while moving toward the cage.
coachNotes: Box lacrosse shooting requires accuracy over power due to the smaller goal and larger goalie equipment.
coachingPoints: Quick release — box goalies take away time
Change planes: catch high, shoot low (and vice versa)
Pick corners — box goals are small, accuracy is everything
Shoot in stride — don't stop your feet
steps: Place a bucket or pylon 5-6 meters in front of the box lacrosse goal
Two lines form at Goal Line Extended on each side
First player scoops a ball from the ground
Runs around the bucket toward center
Shoots while moving toward the cage
Alternate lines
commonMistakes: Stopping to shoot — must shoot on the run
Telegraphing where you're shooting
Not changing planes to beat the goalie

name: Off-Ball Pick-and-Roll (Box Lacrosse)
subtitle: Box lacrosse pick and roll away from the ball
description: Simulates indoor game scenarios where a player sets a pick away from the ball. Once the picker seals the defender, they roll to the net to receive a pass and finish in traffic.
coachNotes: The 2-man game is the foundation of box lacrosse offense.
coachingPoints: Picker must be set — moving picks are penalties
Roll to the net immediately after the ball carrier passes
Ball carrier: use the pick tight — rub shoulders
Look for the slip if defense overplays the pick
steps: Ball carrier on one side, picker on opposite side
2 defenders guard them
Picker sets up and sets a screen for their teammate's defender
After ball carrier uses the pick, picker rolls to the net
Ball carrier feeds the roller for a finish
Rotate roles every 3 reps
commonMistakes: Moving pick — picker must be stationary
Ball carrier not using the pick tight enough
Roller not going hard to the net — must be decisive

name: Inside Arc Finishing (Box Lacrosse)
subtitle: Catch and shoot in one motion off the crease
description: Two lines 12 yards on either side of the box goal. One player cuts on an arc 2-3 yards off the crease while the other feeds them. Cutter must catch and shoot in one motion.
coachNotes: Crease play wins games in box lacrosse. This drill builds the touch needed for tight-space finishing.
coachingPoints: Catch and shoot in one motion — no cradling
Practice dip-and-dunk and behind-the-head fakes
Cut hard on the arc — half-speed cuts don't get open
Change planes to beat the box goalie
steps: Two lines form 12 yards on either side of the goal
One player from line A cuts on a 2-3 yard arc off the crease
Player from line B feeds them a pass
Cutter catches and shoots in one motion
Alternate feeding and cutting lines
Add a defender for advanced reps
commonMistakes: Catching and cradling before shooting — too slow in box
Cutting in a straight line instead of an arc
Not selling a fake before the shot

name: Skeleton Pick and Roll
subtitle: Two-man game foundation drill
description: Set up 4 cones: two at the top and two at GLE. A player drives from the lower cone to accept a pick from a teammate. The picker holds position until the ball carrier passes, then rolls toward the goal for a feed.
coachNotes: The 2-man game is the most important concept in modern lacrosse offense. Run this every practice.
coachingPoints: Picker: set a firm base, feet wider than shoulders, stay stationary
Ball carrier: rub shoulders with the picker — no daylight
Picker: roll hard to the goal after the ball carrier passes
Read the defense — if they switch, feed the roller immediately
steps: Set up 4 cones: 2 at the top (15 yards out), 2 at GLE
Ball carrier starts at a lower cone, picker at the upper cone
Ball carrier drives toward the picker
Picker sets a screen
Ball carrier uses the screen, drives to the cage or passes
Picker rolls to the goal to receive a feed
Finish with a shot
commonMistakes: Moving pick — picker must be completely stationary
Ball carrier not getting tight to the pick
Picker rolling away from the goal instead of toward it

name: Phone Booth 2v2
subtitle: Tight-space 2v2 with picks and quick passes
description: High-intensity 2v2 drill in a confined space. Forces players to use tight picks, quick dump passes, and touch passes to navigate defensive pressure in close quarters.
coachNotes: Great for box lacrosse skills. The confined space mirrors game situations.
coachingPoints: Quick decisions — no time to think in tight space
Use picks and quick touch passes
Protect the stick in traffic
Defenders: communicate switches
steps: Set up a 10x10 yard box near the goal
2 attackers vs 2 defenders inside the box
Play live — attackers try to score
Must stay inside the box boundaries
30-second shot clock
Rotate groups every 2-3 reps
commonMistakes: Overdodging — pass the ball in tight space
Standing still — constant movement is required
Defenders: not communicating switches on picks

name: Three and Go (3v3)
subtitle: Complete 3 passes before attacking the goal
description: Starts as a 3v3 ground ball scenario. Players must complete three passes before attacking the goal. Forces players to spread out and find open lanes before initiating a dodge.
coachNotes: Prevents the one-man show. Teaches ball movement and off-ball activity.
coachingPoints: Three passes before you can dodge or shoot
Off-ball players must move to create passing lanes
Don't force passes — reverse the ball if needed
After 3 passes, attack immediately — don't hesitate
steps: 3v3 inside the offensive zone
Start with a contested ground ball or coach feed
Offensive team must complete 3 passes before shooting
Count passes out loud
After 3 passes, play is live — dodge, shoot, or keep passing
Play until goal, save, or turnover
commonMistakes: Rushing passes to get to 3 — quality passes count
All three players standing still waiting for the ball
Not attacking after completing 3 passes

name: Bogeys Build-Up Drill
subtitle: Progressive 1v1 to 2v2 to 3v3
description: Starts as a 1v1, then builds to 2v2, and finally 3v3 as players enter from the sideline. Teaches how to integrate secondary and tertiary options as complexity increases.
coachNotes: Teaches players to adjust as the situation evolves. Mirrors real game complexity.
coachingPoints: 1v1: attack aggressively — it's your best chance
2v2: look for the 2-man game immediately
3v3: ball movement and off-ball cutting
Defenders: communicate as new players enter
steps: Start with 1v1 at the top of the box
After 5 seconds, a second attacker and defender enter from opposite sides
Now 2v2 — find the 2-man game
After 5 more seconds, third attacker and defender enter
Now 3v3 — play to conclusion
Rotate groups
commonMistakes: Not attacking in the 1v1 phase — waiting for help
New players entering and standing still
Not adjusting strategy as numbers change

name: M-W-8 Stick Check Drill
subtitle: Defenseman stick precision training
description: Hold your stick in athletic stance and trace the letters M, W, and the number 8 around a stationary object (teammate's outstretched arms or a pole) without touching it. Builds muscle memory for complex stick movements.
coachNotes: Underrated drill. Defensive stick skills are as important as offensive ones.
coachingPoints: Precision over speed — don't touch the obstacle
Keep the pattern smooth and controlled
Build speed gradually as muscle memory develops
Use both hands — switch grip
steps: Stand in athletic defensive stance facing a stationary object (pole, teammate, etc.)
Hold long pole in defensive position
Trace the letter 'M' around the object without touching it
Then trace 'W'
Then trace '8' (figure eight)
10 reps of each pattern, increasing speed
commonMistakes: Rushing and hitting the obstacle
Moving feet — stay in defensive stance
Using only one grip pattern

name: Poke Check Drill (Zig-Zag)
subtitle: Throw poke checks while shuffling through cones
description: Shuffle between cones in a zig-zag pattern. At each cone, change direction and throw a controlled poke or lift check while maintaining a low, balanced defensive stance.
coachNotes: Combines footwork with checking. Feet first, stick second — always.
coachingPoints: Poke check: slide stick through top hand like a pool cue
Stay low and balanced — don't lunge forward
Short, sharp motions — no wind-up
Feet stay moving through the entire drill
steps: Set up 6-8 cones in a zig-zag pattern, 5 yards apart
Start at first cone in defensive stance with long pole
Shuffle to the next cone
At each cone, change direction and throw a poke check
Maintain low defensive stance throughout
3-5 reps through the course
commonMistakes: Lunging on the poke check — shifts weight forward
Standing upright between checks
Wind-up before the check — should be quick and hidden

name: Takeaway Teacher
subtitle: Practice precision checks on a swaying target
description: Slide a short stick through a goal's netting to simulate an attacker's cradle. Stand at GLE and throw precise pokes, lifts, and slaps at the swaying stick to build timing and precision.
coachNotes: Solo drill. Can be done before or after practice. Builds the precision for legal, effective checks.
coachingPoints: Aim for the hands and bottom of the stick head
Vary your checks: poke, lift, slap
The target sways — read the movement and time your check
Practice from different angles
steps: Slide a short stick through the goal netting so it hangs and sways
Stand at GLE with your long pole
Throw precision checks at the swaying stick
Practice poke checks, lift checks, and slap checks
10 reps of each type
Move to different angles around the crease
commonMistakes: Wild swinging — be precise
Only practicing one type of check
Standing too close or too far from the target

name: Traffic Light Cradling
subtitle: Youth cradling at varying speeds
description: Coach calls out colors: Red = walk while cradling, Yellow = jog while cradling, Green = sprint while cradling. Teaches maintaining ball control at different speeds.
coachNotes: Perfect for U8-U10. Teaches speed variation while maintaining stick control.
coachingPoints: Keep the stick 'in the box' (near the ear) at all speeds
Cradle tighter and closer to body at higher speeds
Switch hands periodically
steps: All players spread out in a defined area with a ball
Coach calls 'Red!' — everyone walks while cradling
Coach calls 'Yellow!' — everyone jogs
Coach calls 'Green!' — everyone sprints
Vary the calls rapidly to keep kids engaged
If the ball drops, do 3 ground ball pickups before rejoining
commonMistakes: Dropping the stick down at higher speeds
Only cradling with dominant hand

name: Wall Cradle (Verticality Drill)
subtitle: Force vertical stick position against a wall
description: Stand with your back against a wall and cradle. If your stick hits the wall, it's too horizontal. Forces a tight, vertical 'box' cradle essential for stick protection.
coachNotes: Simple but powerful drill for fixing horizontal cradling habits.
coachingPoints: If stick hits the wall, it's too horizontal — tighten up
Keep the stick vertical and close to your ear
This is the cradle you use in traffic
steps: Stand with your back flat against a wall
Cradle the ball side to side
If your stick hits the wall, your cradle is too wide/horizontal
Tighten the cradle until you can move freely without hitting the wall
Practice for 1-2 minutes per hand
commonMistakes: Leaning away from the wall to cheat — stay flat against it
Only practicing dominant hand

name: Mirror Stick Protection
subtitle: Self-check cradle visibility in a mirror
description: Stand in front of a mirror and cradle. If you can see the ball in the mirror, a defender can see and check it. Practice keeping the stick vertical and tucked behind your shoulder.
coachNotes: At-home drill. Builds awareness of stick visibility that translates to game situations.
coachingPoints: If you can see the ball in the mirror, a defender can check it
Keep stick behind your shoulder — use your body as a shield
Practice both hands
steps: Stand in front of a full-length mirror
Hold your stick as if a defender is in front of you (the mirror)
Cradle and move laterally
If you can see the ball in the mirror, adjust your stick position
Goal: the ball should be completely hidden behind your body/shoulder
commonMistakes: Stick too far from the body — tuck it in
Not simulating game movement — move laterally, not just standing

name: Dynamic Warm-Up: World's Greatest Stretch
subtitle: Full-body lacrosse warm-up stretch
description: A deep lunge with a thoracic twist that targets the hamstrings, hips, and upper back simultaneously. Essential component of a lacrosse dynamic warm-up.
coachNotes: Part of the standard dynamic warm-up. Do before every practice and game.
coachingPoints: Deep lunge — back knee close to the ground
Rotate through the thoracic spine, not the lower back
Hold the twist for 2-3 seconds
Move through 10-20 yards
steps: Step forward into a deep lunge
Place the same-side hand on the ground next to your front foot
Rotate your torso and reach the opposite hand toward the sky
Hold for 2-3 seconds
Step forward into the next lunge and repeat on the other side
Cover 10-20 yards
commonMistakes: Shallow lunge — get deep
Rotating from the lower back instead of mid-back
Rushing through it — this is about mobility, not speed

name: Quick Stick Shuttle
subtitle: Rapid-fire partner passing with no cradle
description: Partners stand 5 yards apart and exchange rapid-fire passes without cradling. Focuses on soft hands and quick releases as a stick-work warm-up.
coachNotes: Perfect warm-up drill. Gets hands active quickly.
coachingPoints: No cradling — catch and release immediately
Soft hands on the catch — absorb, don't fight the ball
Aim for your partner's stick-side ear
steps: Partners face each other, 5 yards apart
Pass back and forth as quickly as possible
No cradling between catch and throw
30-second rounds
Switch hands and repeat
commonMistakes: Cradling between catch and throw
Passing too hard at close range
Not switching to weak hand

name: Ball Drop Sprint
subtitle: Reaction sprint with ground ball finish
description: A partner drops a lacrosse ball from shoulder height. You must sprint and scoop it before the second bounce. Builds explosive reaction and ground ball technique.
coachNotes: Combines conditioning with ground ball work. Kids love the competitive element.
coachingPoints: React to the drop, not to anticipation
Sprint low — you need to scoop immediately
Proper ground ball technique even at speed
steps: Partner stands 5-8 yards away holding a ball at shoulder height
Sprinter starts in athletic stance facing the partner
Partner drops the ball (no throwing)
Sprinter reacts and scoops before the second bounce
Increase distance as reaction improves
10 reps per player
commonMistakes: Anticipating the drop — must react to visual cue
Standing too upright during the sprint
Scooping with poor technique due to rushing

name: Flipz Catching Drill
subtitle: Self-toss catching for beginners
description: Toss the ball 4-5 feet into the air yourself. Reach up with your stick to meet the ball and pull it back toward your shoulder as it lands in the pocket. Teaches 'soft hands'.
coachNotes: Best first catching drill for absolute beginners. Eliminates the variable of a partner's throw.
coachingPoints: Reach UP to meet the ball — don't wait for it to fall
Pull the stick back toward your shoulder as the ball lands (give with it)
This 'give' is what 'soft hands' means
steps: Hold your stick in catching position
Toss the ball 4-5 feet straight up with your free hand
Reach up with your stick to meet the ball
As the ball enters the pocket, pull the stick back toward your shoulder
20 catches with right hand, 20 with left
commonMistakes: Waiting for the ball to fall into the stick — reach up
Not pulling back (giving) — ball bounces out
Tossing too high or too far from body

name: Musical Ground Balls
subtitle: Musical chairs but with lacrosse ground balls
description: Like musical chairs, but players must scoop a ball to stay in the game. Scatter fewer balls than players. When music stops (or whistle blows), scramble to scoop a ball. Player without a ball is out.
coachNotes: End-of-practice fun drill. Kids go all-out because of the elimination format.
coachingPoints: Proper scoop technique even in the scramble
Keep your head up — see where the balls are
Be aggressive but controlled
steps: Scatter balls in a defined area (1 fewer ball than players)
Players jog/run around the area while whistle blows (or music plays)
When whistle stops, scramble to scoop a ball
Player without a ball is eliminated
Remove one ball and repeat
Last player standing wins
commonMistakes: Pushing other players — keep it clean
Poor scoop technique in the rush

name: Face Dodge Passing
subtitle: Protect the stick with a face dodge before passing
description: As you receive the ball from a partner, perform a face dodge (pulling the stick across your face to protect it) before passing back. Combines catching, dodging, and passing in one rep.
coachNotes: Combines three skills in one rep. Great for efficiency in practice.
coachingPoints: Sell the dodge — pull the stick dramatically across your face
Don't switch hands during the face dodge
Immediately pass back to your partner after the dodge
steps: Partners face each other, 10 yards apart
Player A passes to Player B
Player B catches, performs a face dodge, then passes back
Player A catches, performs a face dodge, and passes back
Continuous for 2 minutes, then switch to weak hand
commonMistakes: Switching hands during the face dodge — defeats the purpose
Lazy dodge that wouldn't fool a defender
Breaking the rhythm between dodge and pass

name: Clear Through Drill
subtitle: Women's lacrosse off-ball cutting to create space
description: Off-ball players practice cutting through the 8-meter arc to draw their defenders away from the ball carrier, creating a gap for a teammate to dodge and shoot.
coachNotes: The most important off-ball concept in women's lacrosse. When a teammate dodges toward you, clear through to make space.
coachingPoints: When the ball carrier dodges toward you, cut through the 8-meter
Your cut draws your defender away, creating space
Cut with purpose — make it look like you want the ball
Clear to the opposite side of the field
steps: Set up 3v3 or 4v4 around the 8-meter arc
Ball carrier initiates a dodge from the wing
The player closest to the dodge path 'clears through' the arc
Their defender follows, opening space
Ball carrier attacks the vacated space
If the clearing player's defender doesn't follow, they become the open pass target
commonMistakes: Standing still when the ball carrier drives toward you
Cutting toward the ball instead of away (clogs the lane)
Jogging the cut — must sprint to sell it

name: Four-Corner Give and Go (Box Lacrosse)
subtitle: Pass and cut from four corners of the floor
description: Four lines set under the restraining lines. Players pass diagonally across the floor, cut toward the net, and receive a return pass for a quick-release shot.
coachNotes: Box lacrosse fundamental drill. Teaches give-and-go which is the basis of all box offense.
coachingPoints: Pass and immediately cut — don't watch your pass
Cut toward the net at speed
Quick release shot on the return pass
Change planes on the shot
steps: Four lines at the four corners of the box floor
Player from line A passes diagonally to Player from line B
Player A immediately cuts toward the net
Player B returns the pass to cutting Player A
Player A catches and shoots in one motion
Rotate lines
commonMistakes: Watching the pass instead of cutting immediately
Slow cut — must sprint to the net
Catching and cradling instead of quick-release shot

name: Agility Ladder Cradling
subtitle: Ladder footwork patterns while cradling
description: Perform agility ladder patterns (Ickey Shuffle, Two-Feet-In, Lateral Hops) while cradling a ball. Advanced players practice split dodges through the ladder.
coachNotes: Sport-specific conditioning. The stick adds the lacrosse element to standard footwork drills.
coachingPoints: Maintain proper cradle throughout — don't let footwork ruin stick work
Stay on balls of feet — quick, light steps
Keep head up — don't look at the ladder
Progress to split dodges and hand switches through the ladder
steps: Lay out an agility ladder on the field
Perform standard ladder patterns while cradling
Start with: Two-Feet-In, Ickey Shuffle, Lateral Hops
Advanced: split dodge hand switches at every other rung
3 reps of each pattern
Switch hands between reps
commonMistakes: Looking down at the ladder — keep head up
Losing the ball during complex footwork patterns
Sacrificing footwork quality to maintain the cradle (or vice versa)

name: Rapid Fire Relay
subtitle: Team race to complete accurate passes
description: Teams of 4-5 players race to complete 20 accurate passes to a coach or target 10 yards away. Encourages passing under the pressure of a race.
coachNotes: Adds competitive pressure to basic passing. Kids try harder when their team is counting on them.
coachingPoints: Accuracy over speed — dropped passes cost time
Proper form even under pressure
Encourage teammates — it's a team effort
steps: Divide into teams of 4-5 players
Each team lines up 10 yards from a coach or target
On whistle, first player passes to the coach
Coach passes back, player goes to end of line
Next player goes immediately
First team to complete 20 successful catches wins
commonMistakes: Rushing and throwing wild passes
Not being ready when it's your turn
Dropped passes — only completed catches count

name: Over-the-Shoulder Passing
subtitle: Lead pass to a sprinting teammate
description: One partner runs away while the other throws a lead pass over their shoulder, simulating a clear or fast break outlet pass. Teaches timing and pass placement.
coachNotes: Critical skill for clearing and transition. The receiver should never break stride.
coachingPoints: Lead the runner — throw where they're going, not where they are
Receiver: don't slow down or turn around
Passer: throw to the stick side (outside shoulder)
Receiver: reach back and cradle through the catch
steps: Partners start 5 yards apart, facing the same direction
Receiver sprints away at full speed
Passer throws a lead pass over the receiver's outside shoulder
Receiver catches without breaking stride
Continue down the field
Switch roles and return
commonMistakes: Throwing behind the runner
Receiver slowing down to wait for the ball
Throwing to the wrong shoulder (inside = defender can reach it)

name: Trigger Drill (3v3/4v4)
subtitle: Live scrimmage with coached slide triggers
description: A 3v3 or 4v4 scrimmage where offense keeps possession until the coach blows a whistle or a dodger beats their man. This triggers an immediate slide rotation.
coachNotes: Teaches players to recognize the exact moment a slide is necessary versus just hedging.
coachingPoints: Recognize the EXACT moment the slide is needed — not too early, not too late
The difference between hedging (faking) and committing to the slide
Off-ball defenders must rotate immediately when the slide fires
Communication: 'Fire!' when sliding, 'Two!' when covering the next
steps: Set up 3v3 or 4v4 half-field
Offense passes the ball around, looking to dodge
When a dodger beats their defender (or coach blows whistle), it triggers a slide
Defense must execute full slide rotation
If defense recovers, play continues
If offense scores, defense does push-ups
commonMistakes: Sliding too early — tips off the offense
Sliding too late — dodger gets a clean look
Not recovering after the slide — leaves a man open

name: In-and-Out Footwork Drill
subtitle: Defensive approach and retreat pattern
description: Defenders practice sprinting forward 5 yards to close out a phantom attacker, then immediately transition into a drop-step and backpedal. Mimics the approach-and-retreat rhythm of on-ball defense.
coachNotes: Fundamental defensive footwork pattern. Simple but essential.
coachingPoints: Sprint forward to close the gap
Break down into short choppy steps before the 'attacker'
Drop step and backpedal — don't turn your hips
Stay low throughout — don't pop up
steps: Place 2 cones 5 yards apart
Start at cone A in defensive stance
Sprint forward to cone B (closing out)
Break down with choppy steps at cone B
Drop step and backpedal to cone A
Repeat 10 times
Add lateral movement for advanced reps
commonMistakes: Arriving at full speed without breaking down
Turning hips during the retreat — stay square
Standing too tall
